DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.name: Unauthenticated Modbus/TCP write accepted by loopback stub PLC
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.setup: No real hardware or plant network. Save as fieldbus_sim.py. It stands up an in-process SOFTWARE Modbus/TCP stub server bound to 127.0.0.1 and a local client that issues a Write Single Register (FC 0x06). Pure loopback simulation modeling that classic fieldbus protocols carry no source authentication.
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-fieldbus-no-auth SIM: loopback-only Modbus/TCP stub showing the protocol
# accepts a register write with NO authentication. No real PLC, no plant net.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary (a synthetic 'coil' value).
import socket, struct, threading, sys, time

HOST, PORT = "127.0.0.1", 15020  # non-privileged loopback sim port
REGS = {0: 0}  # synthetic holding register; 0 = safe default

def stub_server(ready):
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    s.bind((HOST, PORT)); s.listen(1); ready.set()
    conn, _ = s.accept()
    req = conn.recv(256)
    # Modbus/TCP MBAP: txid(2) proto(2) len(2) unit(1) func(1) addr(2) val(2)
    if len(req) >= 12:
        txid, proto, ln, unit, func = struct.unpack(">HHHBB", req[:8])
        addr, val = struct.unpack(">HH", req[8:12])
        # THE SEAM: no credential/source check -- write is honored as-is
        if func == 0x06:
            REGS[addr] = val
            conn.sendall(req[:12])  # echo = success
    conn.close(); s.close()

def client_write(addr, val):
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    s.connect((HOST, PORT))
    pdu = struct.pack(">HHHBBHH", 1, 0, 6, 1, 0x06, addr, val)
    s.sendall(pdu); resp = s.recv(256); s.close()
    return len(resp) >= 12

def main():
    ready = threading.Event()
    t = threading.Thread(target=stub_server, args=(ready,), daemon=True)
    t.start(); ready.wait(2)
    print("register[0] before (safe default):", REGS[0])
    ok = client_write(0, 0xABCD)  # anonymous, no auth handshake
    time.sleep(0.2)
    print("client write acknowledged:", ok, "| register[0] now:", hex(REGS[0]))
    if ok and REGS[0] == 0xABCD:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Modbus stub honored an UNAUTHENTICATED write -- "
              "no source auth in the fieldbus protocol"); sys.exit(0)
    print("[no write]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.success_criterion: Output shows register[0] starting at 0, the client write acknowledged True, register[0] now 0xabcd, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the loopback stub accepted a register write with no authentication, demonstrating the fieldbus has no source auth.
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.cleanup: Process exits on completion; nothing persists. rm fieldbus_sim.py
DOM-fieldbus-no-auth.safety: Pure loopback simulation against an in-process software stub; no live transmit, no real PLC, no plant-network attach, no actuation.

DOM-ews-logic-download.name: Unauthenticated PLC logic download accepted by stub engineering controller
DOM-ews-logic-download.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-ews-logic-download.setup: No real PLC. Save as ews_logic_sim.py. It models an engineering-workstation (EWS) to PLC program-download session as a SOFTWARE state machine that does not require any engineering password/session auth before replacing the control logic.
DOM-ews-logic-download.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-ews-logic-download SIM: software model of a PLC accepting a logic download
# with NO engineering authentication. Pure in-memory state machine.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary logic blob.
import hashlib, sys

class StubPLC:
    def __init__(self):
        self.mode = "RUN"
        self.logic = "BASE_PROGRAM_v1"  # benign installed program
        self.auth_required = False      # THE SEAM: vendor protocol has no auth
    def stop(self):
        self.mode = "STOP"
    def download(self, blob, credential=None):
        # No credential check is performed -- session is implicitly trusted
        if self.auth_required and credential is None:
            return False
        self.logic = blob
        return True

def main():
    plc = StubPLC()
    print("installed logic:", plc.logic, "| mode:", plc.mode)
    plc.stop()  # EWS commands STOP, then pushes new program -- no login
    new_blob = "CANARY_PROGRAM_v2"  # synthetic replacement logic
    ok = plc.download(new_blob, credential=None)
    h = hashlib.sha256(plc.logic.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
    print("download accepted (no credential):", ok,
          "| running logic:", plc.logic, "| sha:", h)
    if ok and plc.logic == new_blob:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] PLC replaced its control logic via an "
              "UNAUTHENTICATED download -- no engineering session auth")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[download rejected]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-ews-logic-download.success_criterion: Output shows the base program loaded, download accepted True with credential=None, running logic now CANARY_PROGRAM_v2, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the stub PLC accepted a logic download without any engineering authentication.
DOM-ews-logic-download.cleanup: In-memory only; process exit clears state. rm ews_logic_sim.py
DOM-ews-logic-download.safety: Pure in-memory simulation of a controller state machine; no real PLC, no download to live equipment, no actuation.

DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.name: Spoofed sensor value suppresses safety trip in stub SIS logic
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.setup: No real safety system. Save as sis_spoof_sim.py. It models a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) trip function as SOFTWARE that reads an unauthenticated sensor value and decides whether to trip. Demonstrates that a spoofed (forged) sensor reading suppresses the safe-trip.
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-sis-sensor-spoof SIM: software SIS trip-logic model fed an unauthenticated
# sensor value. A forged in-range reading suppresses the safe shutdown.
# Pure simulation -- no real sensor, no real final element / actuation.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary scenario.
import sys

TRIP_SETPOINT = 80.0  # e.g. degrees C; above this the SIS must trip to SAFE

def sis_evaluate(sensor_value, authenticated):
    # THE SEAM: trip decision trusts the raw value; no integrity/auth on input
    if sensor_value >= TRIP_SETPOINT:
        return "TRIP_TO_SAFE"
    return "NORMAL_RUN"

def main():
    true_value = 95.0   # real process is over the limit -> should trip
    print("true process value:", true_value, "setpoint:", TRIP_SETPOINT)
    honest = sis_evaluate(true_value, authenticated=True)
    print("honest SIS decision:", honest)
    spoofed_value = 60.0  # attacker forges an in-range reading on the bus
    result = sis_evaluate(spoofed_value, authenticated=False)
    print("spoofed sensor value injected:", spoofed_value,
          "| SIS decision:", result)
    if honest == "TRIP_TO_SAFE" and result == "NORMAL_RUN":
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Spoofed unauthenticated sensor value SUPPRESSED "
              "the safe-trip -- SIS input has no integrity/authentication")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[trip not suppressed]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.success_criterion: Output shows honest decision TRIP_TO_SAFE at true value 95, then with the spoofed 60.0 reading the SIS decision is NORMAL_RUN, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- a forged sensor value suppressed the safe trip because the input is unauthenticated.
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.cleanup: In-memory only; process exit clears state. rm sis_spoof_sim.py
DOM-sis-sensor-spoof.safety: Pure simulation of trip logic; no real sensor, no SIS hardware, no final-element actuation.

DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.name: Re-signed APK trusted by stub installer (signing identity not pinned)
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.setup: No real phone or app store. Save as repackage_audit.py. It models APK signing-cert verification as a SOFTWARE check: a sample app has an original signer cert digest; the script simulates re-signing with a different developer key and shows the stub installer trusts it because it only validates that the package IS signed, not WHICH key signed it. Local audit, no distribution.
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-mobile-repackaged-app AUDIT/SIM: models a mobile installer that verifies
# 'is the APK signed?' but does NOT pin the signing certificate. A repackaged
# app re-signed with a different key is therefore trusted. No real APK, no
# install, no distribution. AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic certs.
import hashlib, sys

def cert_digest(key_material):
    return hashlib.sha256(key_material.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

# Synthetic signing identities (NOT real keys)
ORIG_KEY = "vendor-release-key"
ATTACKER_KEY = "self-signed-rogue-key"
EXPECTED_SIGNER = None  # THE SEAM: installer pins no expected signer

def installer_trusts(apk_signer_digest, expected=EXPECTED_SIGNER):
    if apk_signer_digest is None:
        return False  # unsigned rejected
    if expected is None:
        return True   # 'any valid signature' accepted -> repackaging works
    return apk_signer_digest == expected

def main():
    orig = cert_digest(ORIG_KEY)
    repkg = cert_digest(ATTACKER_KEY)
    print("original signer:", orig)
    print("repackaged signer (different key):", repkg)
    trusted = installer_trusts(repkg)
    print("installer trusts repackaged app:", trusted,
          "| signer pinned:", EXPECTED_SIGNER is not None)
    if trusted and repkg != orig:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Installer trusts a re-signed/repackaged app "
              "under a DIFFERENT key -- no signing-identity pinning")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[repackage rejected]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.success_criterion: Output shows distinct original and repackaged signer digests, installer trusts repackaged app True with signer pinned False, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the installer accepts any valid signature, so a repackaged/re-signed app is trusted.
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.cleanup: In-memory only; process exit clears state. rm repackage_audit.py
DOM-mobile-repackaged-app.safety: Local static/logic audit with synthetic certs; no real APK signing, no install, no app-store distribution.

DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.name: MDM profile audit flags over-broad device-control grants
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.setup: No real device enrollment. Save as mdm_profile_audit.py. It parses a SAMPLE MDM/configuration-profile (inline synthetic plist-like dict) and audits which control payloads it grants, flagging an over-broad profile that yields full device control (VPN capture, cert install, app install, remote wipe, supervised).
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse AUDIT: parse a SAMPLE MDM profile and flag
# over-broad device-control grants. Static, offline, no enrollment, no device.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic profile.
import sys

# Synthetic MDM profile (models payloads a malicious/over-permissioned profile holds)
SAMPLE_PROFILE = {
    "PayloadDisplayName": "Corp Onboarding",
    "Supervised": True,
    "Payloads": [
        "com.apple.vpn.managed",          # can route/capture traffic
        "com.apple.security.root",        # install trusted root CA
        "com.apple.applicationaccess",    # restrictions / control
        "com.apple.mdm.install_app",      # push apps silently
        "com.apple.mdm.erase_device",     # remote wipe
    ],
}

HIGH_RISK = {
    "com.apple.vpn.managed": "traffic interception",
    "com.apple.security.root": "trust forged TLS certs",
    "com.apple.mdm.install_app": "silent app push",
    "com.apple.mdm.erase_device": "remote wipe",
}

def audit(profile):
    flags = [(p, HIGH_RISK[p]) for p in profile["Payloads"] if p in HIGH_RISK]
    return flags

def main():
    print("profile:", SAMPLE_PROFILE["PayloadDisplayName"],
          "| supervised:", SAMPLE_PROFILE["Supervised"])
    flags = audit(SAMPLE_PROFILE)
    for p, why in flags:
        print("  HIGH-RISK GRANT:", p, "->", why)
    if flags and SAMPLE_PROFILE["Supervised"]:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] MDM profile grants over-broad control "
              "(%d high-risk payloads) enabling full device takeover" % len(flags))
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[profile within bounds]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.success_criterion: Output lists multiple HIGH-RISK GRANT lines (VPN capture, root CA, silent app push, remote wipe) on a Supervised profile and prints '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the audit flagged an over-broad MDM profile that enables device takeover.
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.cleanup: Static script, no state. rm mdm_profile_audit.py
DOM-mobile-mdm-profile-abuse.safety: Offline static audit of a synthetic profile; no device enrollment, no MDM server, no real configuration applied.

DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.name: Firmware image audit flags unsigned image and version downgrade
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.setup: No real device flashing. Save as fw_downgrade_audit.py. It builds a SYNTHETIC firmware header (version + signature field) and audits it: confirms there is no valid signature and that the update policy permits installing a lower version than currently running (anti-rollback disabled).
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade AUDIT: inspect a SYNTHETIC firmware header for a
# missing signature and a permitted version downgrade (no anti-rollback).
# Offline file/struct audit only -- no device, no flashing.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic image.
import sys

INSTALLED_VERSION = 5  # currently running firmware version

# Synthetic candidate firmware header
CANDIDATE = {
    "version": 2,            # LOWER than installed -> downgrade
    "signature": b"",       # empty -> unsigned
    "min_version_policy": 0, # device accepts any version >= 0 (no rollback guard)
}

def audit(installed, cand):
    unsigned = (len(cand["signature"]) == 0)
    downgrade_allowed = (cand["version"] >= cand["min_version_policy"]
                         and cand["version"] < installed)
    return unsigned, downgrade_allowed

def main():
    unsigned, downgrade = audit(INSTALLED_VERSION, CANDIDATE)
    print("installed version:", INSTALLED_VERSION,
          "| candidate version:", CANDIDATE["version"])
    print("image unsigned:", unsigned,
          "| downgrade permitted (anti-rollback off):", downgrade)
    if unsigned and downgrade:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Firmware image is UNSIGNED and a version "
              "DOWNGRADE is permitted -- no signature + no anti-rollback")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[image acceptable]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.success_criterion: Output shows installed version 5, candidate 2, image unsigned True, downgrade permitted True, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the audit flagged an unsigned image plus an allowed rollback.
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.cleanup: Static script, no state. rm fw_downgrade_audit.py
DOM-fw-unsigned-downgrade.safety: Offline audit of a synthetic firmware header; no device, no flashing, no transmit.

DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.name: Boot-config audit flags secure boot disabled / verification skipped
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.setup: No real device. Save as secureboot_audit.py. It parses a SAMPLE boot configuration and flags that the secure-boot enforcement flag is off (or set to log-only), so an unsigned/modified boot stage would be loaded.
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass AUDIT: inspect a SAMPLE boot config and flag that
# secure boot is disabled / verification is non-enforcing. Offline config
# audit -- no device, no flashing. AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic config.
import sys

# Synthetic boot configuration
BOOTCFG = {
    "secure_boot_enabled": False,    # enforcement off
    "verify_mode": "log-only",       # even if 'on', would not halt on failure
    "trusted_keys_provisioned": True,
    "next_stage_signature_valid": False,  # next stage is unsigned/modified
}

def audit(cfg):
    enforcing = cfg["secure_boot_enabled"] and cfg["verify_mode"] == "enforce"
    would_boot_untrusted = (not enforcing) and not cfg["next_stage_signature_valid"]
    return enforcing, would_boot_untrusted

def main():
    enforcing, untrusted = audit(BOOTCFG)
    print("secure boot enforcing:", enforcing,
          "| verify mode:", BOOTCFG["verify_mode"])
    print("next stage signature valid:", BOOTCFG["next_stage_signature_valid"],
          "| would boot untrusted stage:", untrusted)
    if untrusted:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Secure boot is NOT enforcing -- an unsigned/"
              "modified boot stage would be loaded (verification bypassed)")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[secure boot enforcing]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.success_criterion: Output shows secure boot enforcing False, verify mode log-only, next stage signature valid False, would boot untrusted stage True, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the audit flagged a non-enforcing secure boot that would load an unsigned stage.
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.cleanup: Static script, no state. rm secureboot_audit.py
DOM-fw-secureboot-bypass.safety: Offline audit of a synthetic boot config; no device, no flashing, no boot manipulation.

DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.name: Documented shielded-bench voltage/clock glitch secret-extraction procedure
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.setup: Read-only documentation. A dedicated, authorized hardware-security bench with an OWNED sacrificial development board, a glitch tool (e.g., ChipWhisperer), bench PSU, and a written RoE. No production device, no third-party hardware.
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.script:
DOCUMENTED SHIELDED-BENCH PROCEDURE -- fault-injection (glitch) secret-extraction seam.
This is a PROCEDURE description for an authorized hardware-security lab. It contains
NO exploit code, NO transmit, and must run ONLY on an owned sacrificial board.

PURPOSE: Demonstrate the seam that a device's secret-read or debug-lock check can
be skipped by a voltage/clock fault, allowing key/secret extraction -- to justify
fault-resistant design (redundant checks, sensors, authenticated debug).

PRECONDITIONS (all required):
  1. Signed RoE naming the exact sacrificial development board (owned by the lab).
  2. The board is NOT a production/customer device; data on it is synthetic canary.
  3. Work performed inside the hardware lab; no RF emission involved.

PROCEDURE (high level, defanged):
  1. Provision the dev board with a SYNTHETIC canary secret (e.g., 0xC0FFEE...),
     not a real key. Record its expected protected state (debug locked).
  2. Characterize timing: identify the window where the firmware evaluates the
     debug-lock / secret-read-protect check.
  3. Using the glitch tool on the OWNED board only, sweep glitch parameters
     (offset, width, voltage drop) against that window.
  4. OBSERVE whether the check is skipped (debug unlocks / canary secret becomes
     readable). Log the glitch parameters that succeed.
  5. SUCCESS = the synthetic canary secret is recovered, proving the protection
     check is not fault-hardened.

EXPLICIT PROHIBITIONS:
  - Do NOT perform on any device you do not own / are not authorized to destroy.
  - Do NOT extract real keys or customer data -- canary only.
  - No network, no RF, no transmit.

REPORTING: Record success glitch parameters + recommend countermeasures
(redundant checks, glitch detectors, authenticated debug, key zeroization).
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.success_criterion: On the OWNED sacrificial board, the synthetic canary secret is recovered or debug unlocks after a glitch, confirming the protection check is not fault-hardened. Documented as a procedure outcome -- no code executed here.
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.cleanup: Power down and isolate the sacrificial board; zeroize/erase the canary secret; archive the bench log per RoE.
DOM-fw-fault-glitch-extract.safety: Documented shielded-bench procedure (text only); owned sacrificial board, canary secrets, no live transmit, no RF, no production device.

DOM-can-frame-injection.name: Unauthenticated CAN frame accepted on vcan loopback stub ECU
DOM-can-frame-injection.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-can-frame-injection.setup: No real vehicle or CAN bus. Requires Linux SocketCAN with a virtual vcan interface and can-utils (cansend/candump). The script self-SKIPs cleanly if vcan or tools are absent. It models that classic CAN has no per-frame authentication: a frame sent on the loopback vcan is received and 'accepted' by a stub consumer.
DOM-can-frame-injection.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DOM-can-frame-injection SIM: virtual-CAN (vcan) LOOPBACK only. Shows classic
# CAN accepts an unauthenticated frame. Self-SKIPs if vcan/can-utils absent.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- no real vehicle, no physical CAN, no actuation.
set -u
IFACE="vcan0"

# --- capability checks: skip cleanly rather than touching anything real ---
command -v cansend >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "[SKIP] can-utils (cansend) not installed"; exit 0; }
command -v candump >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "[SKIP] can-utils (candump) not installed"; exit 0; }
if ! ip link show "$IFACE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "[SKIP] virtual interface $IFACE not present. To create (requires privilege):"
  echo "       sudo modprobe vcan; sudo ip link add dev $IFACE type vcan; sudo ip link set up $IFACE"
  exit 0
fi

echo "[*] Listening on $IFACE (loopback) ..."
LOG="$(mktemp)"
candump -T 2000 "$IFACE" > "$LOG" 2>/dev/null &
DUMP_PID=$!
sleep 0.3

# THE SEAM: a benign synthetic frame, no auth/MAC, is sent and will be received.
CANARY_ID="123"
CANARY_DATA="DEADBEEF"
echo "[*] Injecting unauthenticated canary frame ${CANARY_ID}#${CANARY_DATA}"
cansend "$IFACE" "${CANARY_ID}#${CANARY_DATA}"
sleep 0.5
kill "$DUMP_PID" 2>/dev/null; wait "$DUMP_PID" 2>/dev/null

if grep -qi "$CANARY_DATA" "$LOG"; then
  echo "[*] Frame received on bus:"; grep -i "$CANARY_DATA" "$LOG"
  echo "[SEAM CONFIRMED] vcan accepted an UNAUTHENTICATED frame -- classic CAN has no per-frame auth"
  rm -f "$LOG"; exit 0
fi
echo "[no frame observed]"; rm -f "$LOG"; exit 1
DOM-can-frame-injection.success_criterion: On a host with vcan0 + can-utils, candump shows the injected 123#DEADBEEF canary frame and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0; on hosts without them the script prints [SKIP] and exits 0 without touching anything.
DOM-can-frame-injection.cleanup: candump process is killed and the temp log removed by the script. To remove the virtual interface: sudo ip link del vcan0 . rm the script.
DOM-can-frame-injection.safety: Virtual-CAN loopback simulation only; no real vehicle, no physical CAN bus, no ECU actuation, self-SKIPs when vcan absent.

DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.name: Relay/rolling-code replay accepted by stub PKES receiver (sim)
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.setup: No radios, no vehicle, no RF. Save as pkes_relay_sim.py. It is a PURE software model of a passive-keyless-entry (PKES) / rolling-code receiver. It demonstrates two seams in-software: (a) a relayed challenge-response succeeds because the receiver has no distance bound, and (b) a captured-but-unused rolling code is replayed within the receiver's forward window.
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode SIM: pure-software model of a keyless-entry receiver.
# Shows (a) relay attack works (no distance bound) and (b) a captured rolling
# code replays inside the forward window. NO radio, NO RF, NO vehicle.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic codes, benign canary unlock flag.
import sys

class StubReceiver:
    def __init__(self):
        self.counter = 100          # last accepted rolling-code counter
        self.window = 256           # forward acceptance window
        self.distance_bound = False # THE SEAM (a): no timing/distance check
    def challenge_response(self, resp_valid, round_trip_ms):
        # Relay just forwards the legit response; no distance bound to catch it
        if self.distance_bound and round_trip_ms > 5:
            return False
        return resp_valid
    def rolling_code(self, code_counter):
        # THE SEAM (b): accepts any counter ahead within the window, once
        if self.counter < code_counter <= self.counter + self.window:
            self.counter = code_counter
            return True
        return False

def main():
    rx = StubReceiver()
    # (a) Relay attack: attacker forwards a valid response with large latency
    relayed = rx.challenge_response(resp_valid=True, round_trip_ms=120)
    print("relay challenge-response accepted:", relayed,
          "| distance bound:", rx.distance_bound)
    # (b) Replay a captured-but-unused future rolling code
    captured = 150  # sniffed code the legitimate fob never used yet
    replayed = rx.rolling_code(captured)
    print("rolling-code replay accepted (counter ->", rx.counter, "):", replayed)
    if relayed and replayed:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] PKES receiver accepted a RELAYED response and a "
              "REPLAYED rolling code -- no distance bound, forgiving window")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[rejected]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.success_criterion: Output shows relay challenge-response accepted True with distance bound False, rolling-code replay accepted True (counter advanced to 150), and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the stub receiver fell to both relay and rolling-code replay in simulation.
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.cleanup: In-memory only; process exit clears state. rm pkes_relay_sim.py
DOM-pkes-relay-rollcode.safety: Pure software simulation; no radio, no RF emission, no vehicle, no key fob, no transmit.

DOM-telematics-ota-remote.name: Stub OTA endpoint accepts an unsigned remote update (sim)
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.setup: No real telematics unit or network. Save as ota_endpoint_sim.py. It models a telematics OTA update handler as a SOFTWARE function (in-process, no sockets) that installs an update package without verifying a signature, accepting an attacker-supplied unsigned image.
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-telematics-ota-remote SIM: in-process model of a telematics OTA handler
# that installs an UNSIGNED update. No network, no real TCU, no flashing.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic update package, benign canary.
import hashlib, sys

class TelematicsOTA:
    def __init__(self):
        self.installed = "tcu-fw-1.0"
        self.require_signature = False  # THE SEAM: no signature verification
    def apply_update(self, pkg):
        sig_ok = pkg.get("signature") not in (None, b"", "")
        if self.require_signature and not sig_ok:
            return False
        self.installed = pkg["image"]
        return True

def main():
    ota = TelematicsOTA()
    print("installed firmware:", ota.installed,
          "| signature required:", ota.require_signature)
    # Attacker-supplied package delivered to the OTA endpoint, unsigned
    rogue = {"image": "tcu-fw-CANARY", "signature": ""}
    ok = ota.apply_update(rogue)
    h = hashlib.sha256(ota.installed.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
    print("update accepted (unsigned):", ok,
          "| installed now:", ota.installed, "| sha:", h)
    if ok and ota.installed == "tcu-fw-CANARY":
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] OTA endpoint installed an UNSIGNED remote update "
              "-- no package signature verification")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[update rejected]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.success_criterion: Output shows installed tcu-fw-1.0 with signature required False, update accepted True for an unsigned package, installed now tcu-fw-CANARY, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the OTA stub accepted an unsigned update.
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.cleanup: In-memory only; process exit clears state. rm ota_endpoint_sim.py
DOM-telematics-ota-remote.safety: In-process simulation of an OTA handler; no network, no real telematics unit, no flashing, no transmit.

DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.name: Receive-only WPA handshake audit (capture completeness + offline-crack exposure)
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.setup: No radio, no capture device, no transmit, no deauth. Save as wpa_handshake_audit.py. It RECEIVE-ONLY inspects a SYNTHETIC parsed 4-way-handshake record (which EAPOL messages were observed and whether the PSK is weak/in a wordlist) and reports whether the captured handshake is crackable offline. No actual cracking is performed; it is an exposure audit.
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-wpa-handshake-crack AUDIT: receive-only inspection of a SYNTHETIC parsed
# WPA 4-way handshake record. Reports OFFLINE-CRACK EXPOSURE. Performs NO actual
# cracking, NO capture, NO transmit/deauth. AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY.
import sys

# Synthetic record as if parsed from a recorded .pcap (no radio used)
HANDSHAKE = {
    "ssid": "LabNet",
    "eapol_msgs_seen": [1, 2, 3, 4],   # complete 4-way handshake captured
    "psk_in_common_wordlist": True,    # weak PSK present in standard wordlists
}

def audit(hs):
    complete = set([1, 2]).issubset(set(hs["eapol_msgs_seen"]))  # M1+M2 enough
    crackable = complete and hs["psk_in_common_wordlist"]
    return complete, crackable

def main():
    complete, crackable = audit(HANDSHAKE)
    print("ssid:", HANDSHAKE["ssid"],
          "| handshake msgs seen:", HANDSHAKE["eapol_msgs_seen"])
    print("capture sufficient for offline attack:", complete,
          "| weak PSK (in wordlist):", HANDSHAKE["psk_in_common_wordlist"])
    if crackable:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] Captured handshake is OFFLINE-CRACKABLE -- "
              "sufficient EAPOL frames + weak PSK (no actual cracking done)")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[not crackable from this record]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.success_criterion: Output shows the EAPOL messages seen, capture sufficient True, weak PSK True, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the receive-only audit reports offline-crack exposure without performing any cracking, capture, or transmit.
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.cleanup: Static script, no state. rm wpa_handshake_audit.py
DOM-wpa-handshake-crack.safety: Receive-only exposure audit over a synthetic record; no radio, no capture, no deauth/transmit, no key recovery performed.

DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.name: Receive-only BLE/RFID relay & clone exposure decode (sim/synthetic)
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.setup: No radio, no reader, no card, no transmit. Save as ble_rfid_relay_audit.py. It RECEIVE-ONLY analyzes SYNTHETIC observation records for (a) a BLE proximity-unlock exchange relayed with high latency that the receiver still accepts, and (b) a static-UID RFID tag whose identifier can be cloned because there is no challenge-response. Pure decode/audit; nothing is emitted.
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone DETECT/SIM: receive-only analysis of SYNTHETIC
# BLE/RFID observation records. Flags (a) BLE relay (no latency bound) and
# (b) RFID clonability (static UID, no challenge-response). NO radio, NO reader,
# NO transmit. AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- synthetic data.
import sys

BLE_EVENT = {"rssi_proximity_unlock": True, "round_trip_ms": 95,
             "latency_bound_ms": None}  # None => no distance/latency check
RFID_TAG  = {"uid": "04A1B2C3", "challenge_response": False}  # static UID only

def ble_relay_works(ev):
    if ev["latency_bound_ms"] is None:
        return ev["rssi_proximity_unlock"]  # accepts even relayed/high-latency
    return ev["round_trip_ms"] <= ev["latency_bound_ms"]

def rfid_clonable(tag):
    return not tag["challenge_response"]  # static UID can be copied verbatim

def main():
    ble = ble_relay_works(BLE_EVENT)
    rfid = rfid_clonable(RFID_TAG)
    print("BLE proximity unlock accepts relayed exchange:", ble,
          "(latency bound:", BLE_EVENT["latency_bound_ms"], ")")
    print("RFID tag clonable (static UID, no challenge-response):", rfid,
          "| uid:", RFID_TAG["uid"])
    if ble and rfid:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] BLE relay accepted (no latency bound) and RFID "
              "UID is clonable (no challenge-response) -- decode-only finding")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[not vulnerable in this record]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.success_criterion: Output shows BLE relay accepted True (latency bound None) and RFID clonable True (static UID, no challenge-response), with '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the receive-only decode flagged relay and clone exposure without emitting anything.
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.cleanup: Static script, no state. rm ble_rfid_relay_audit.py
DOM-ble-rfid-relay-clone.safety: Receive-only decode of synthetic records; no radio, no reader, no card, no relay hardware, no transmit.

DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.name: Documented shielded-bench procedure: deauth/jam/replay (do-NOT-radiate)
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.authorization: Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/phone/open-air RF; sim/receive-only/shielded-bench.
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.setup: Read-only documentation. An authorized RF test environment: an RF-shielded enclosure / Faraday tent or fully wired (coax/attenuator) setup, OWNED test endpoints, a spectrum analyzer for receive-only verification, and a signed RoE. NO open-air emission.
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.script:
DOCUMENTED SHIELDED-BENCH PROCEDURE -- deauth / jamming / replay availability seam.
This is a PROCEDURE description. It contains NO transmit/attack code. Any signal
generation occurs ONLY inside an RF-shielded enclosure or wired/attenuated path,
on OWNED endpoints, under a signed RoE. Radiating in open air is PROHIBITED and
may be illegal.

PURPOSE: Demonstrate that an unauthenticated management/control RF protocol is
vulnerable to availability attacks (802.11 deauth, narrowband jamming) and to
replay -- to justify management-frame protection (802.11w/PMF), authenticated
commands, and frequency-hopping/anti-jam design.

PRECONDITIONS (all required):
  1. RF-shielded enclosure / Faraday tent verified to contain emissions, OR a
     fully wired coax + attenuator setup with no antenna radiating to open air.
  2. All endpoints are OWNED lab devices on synthetic/canary data.
  3. Signed RoE; spectrum analyzer confirms no leakage outside the enclosure.

PROCEDURE (high level, defanged -- no code, no parameters):
  PART A (receive-only first): With the spectrum analyzer / SDR in RECEIVE-ONLY
     mode, record baseline traffic between the owned endpoints inside the shield.
  PART B (deauth, shielded): Using a lab tool restricted to the shielded path,
     observe that injecting unauthenticated 802.11 deauthentication frames drops
     the owned client -- confirming missing management-frame protection.
  PART C (jamming, shielded): Within the enclosure only, observe that broadband
     noise on the channel denies the owned link -- availability impact.
  PART D (replay, shielded): Capture a benign canary control frame and re-emit it
     inside the shield; observe the owned receiver accepts it -- no anti-replay.
  In every part, the analyzer must confirm emissions stay inside the enclosure.

EXPLICIT PROHIBITIONS:
  - Do NOT radiate in open air. Do NOT touch any third-party/production device,
    spectrum, or network. Do NOT jam real channels.
  - All RF stays inside the shielded/wired path; verify containment continuously.

REPORTING: Note which protections were absent (PMF/802.11w, anti-replay,
anti-jam) and recommend remediation. Receive-only captures are the evidence.
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.success_criterion: Inside the shielded/wired path on owned endpoints: the deauth drops the owned client (no PMF), the jam denies the owned link, and a replayed canary control frame is accepted (no anti-replay) -- each verified receive-only, with the analyzer confirming no open-air leakage. Documented as a procedure outcome; no code executed here.
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.cleanup: Power down emitters, confirm spectrum analyzer shows no residual emission, restore endpoints, and archive the receive-only captures and bench log per RoE.
DOM-rf-deauth-jam-replay.safety: Documented shielded-bench procedure (text only); all RF contained in a Faraday/wired path, owned endpoints, receive-only verification, no open-air transmit, no third-party devices.
